79. Gender parity in access to elected and political office has become a constitutional principle, as a result of the amendment of article 7 of the Constitution in November 2007, equal access for women and men to mandates and functions has been established.

80. These provisions were reinforced by the adoption of a law of 28 May 2010 establishing absolute parity in wholly or partially elected bodies such as the National Assembly and the regional and municipal councils.
81. The situation of women has changed considerably in recent years and forced marriage was prohibited in 2001 by article 17 of the Constitution.

82. In addition, Senegalese women can transmit their nationality to their children and the conditions for their access to that nationality are facilitated for their non-Senegal husband, as set out in Act No. 2013-05 of 8 July 2013.
83. In order to further improve the status of women, new sectoral policies are being implemented to combat female genital mutilation and other forms of violence against women and girls.

84. This is achieved through the adoption of a programme to combat gender-based violence and the promotion of human rights for the period 2013-2016, supported by UN Women, the High Commission for Human Rights, the United Nations Population Fund and UNESCO. Moreover, this programme follows the adoption in 2010 of the second national action plan for accelerating the abandonment of female circumcision.

85. The demographic and health survey showed that the rate of national practice of female circumcision increased from 28 per cent in 2004 to 26 per cent in 2013, as reflected in the 5th multiple indicator report in Senegal.

86. The State of Senegal also distinguished itself very early in the creation of sustainable mechanisms to step up the fight against violations of human rights including torture. The death penalty was abolished by national representation on the symbolic date of 10 December 2004.

87. The issue of child protection is also a priority for the State of Senegal. In this regard, the State has included the theme of combating trafficking in children among the priorities of the National Strategy for Economic and Social Development. Measures are being taken to put an end to their exploitation and programmes are being initiated for their removal from the street and their social inclusion, with increased monitoring of the movement of children along border areas and on roads (Act No. 2005-06 on trafficking in persons and similar practices and the protection of victims). Thus, a unit to combat trafficking in persons has been set up.

88. The national plan of action to combat trafficking in children is continuing and the State, through the Ministry of National Education and the Ministry of Women, Family and Children, has set up several projects to improve the reception and life of children attending so-called " Daaras" Koranic schools.

90. At the level of Parliament, a Law and Human Rights Commission has been established and parliamentary networks at the subregional level are organized in the framework of the promotion and protection of human rights.
91. In addition, a number of regulatory bodies have been established, particularly in the field of audiovisual and election supervision.

92. The National Council for the Regulation of Audiovisual Affairs (CNRA), established by Act No. 200604 of 4 January 2006, is responsible for ensuring the cohesion of the audiovisual sector and ensuring respect for the rules of pluralism, ethics, ethics, laws and regulations in force, as well as the specifications and conventions governing the media. This new institution updates and strengthens the audiovisual system in force in Senegal since 1991 by, inter alia, abolishing the former High Council for Audiovisual Affairs (HCA).

93. The National Autonomous Electoral Commission (CENA) set up by Act No. 2005-07 of 11 May 2005 ensures that the electoral law is applied by both administrative authorities and political parties, candidates and voters, and has important prerogatives to ensure free and transparent elections. The digitization of national and voter identification cards and the publication of electoral lists, particularly on the Internet, further facilitates the monitoring of elections.

94. Pursuant to United Nations General Assembly Resolution 59/113B on the Plan of Action for the first phase (2005-2007) of the World Programme for Human Rights Education in Primary and Secondary Education Systems, the Government of Senegal, through the Ministry of Education in close collaboration with civil society organizations, has developed a curriculum in the field of human rights education in basic education, which has been the subject of consultations at the national level with the support of development partners, in particular the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO).

It has been established to promote the application of international human rights conventions at the national level, and to deal with petitions and other inquiries alleging violations of human rights. It also prepares periodic reports of the Government of Senegal to the international treaty bodies with the expertise of the National Advisory Council on Human Rights.

It also provides advice and proposals to the Government in the field of human rights, international humanitarian law and humanitarian action.

items_fr: 107. Le Comité Sénégalais des Droits de l'Homme est l'institution nationale des droits de l'homme. Sa création,  dès  1970,  s'inscrit  dans  une  perspective  de  lutte  contre  toutes  les formes de discrimination portant atteinte à la dignité humaine.
items_en: 107. The Senegalese Committee on Human Rights is the national human rights institution. Its creation, in 1970, is part of a plan to combat all forms of discrimination that violate human dignity.

items_fr: 108. La Loi suprême du pays fait du pouvoir judiciaire, la gardienne des droits et libertés qu'elle  consacre.  L'aménagement  judiciaire  des  droits  de  la  personne  humaine  concerne surtout la haute juridiction Constitutionnelle et les juridictions de droit commun.

109. Dans son œuvre créatrice de  droits et  obligations,  le  législateur,  comme  l'autorité administrative,  peut  bien  poser  des  limites  au  contenu  ou  aux  conditions  d'exercice  de dispositions légales relatives aux droits de la personne humaine. Dans ces cas, le Conseil Constitutionnel peut être saisi aux fins d'empêcher la promulgation ou l'application de la loi suivant deux procédures notamment par voie d'action et par voie d'exception :

· La saisine  par  voie  d'action :  la  Constitution  du  Sénégal  donne  à  Monsieur  le Président  de  la  République  ou  aux  députés  représentant  1/10 e des  membres  de l'Assemblée Nationale, le pouvoir de saisir le Conseil Constitutionnel d'un recours visant  à  faire  contrôler  la  conformité  d'une  loi  à  la  Constitution  avant  sa promulgation. Ainsi, une loi qui méconnaît des dispositions relatives aux droits de la personne humaine peut être déférée devant le Conseil Constitutionnel aux fins d'un tel contrôle.

· La saisine par voie d'exception : le constituant a entendu « démocratiser » l'accès au  Conseil  Constitutionnel  pour  un  contrôle  de  la  Constitutionnalité  de  la  loi,  au moyen du mécanisme de saisine par voie d'exception. Cette saisine est en effet plus ouverte,  en  ce  sens  qu'elle  est  à  la  portée  de  tout  justiciable  qui  peut,  sous  les conditions prévues par la loi, soulever l'exception d'inconstitutionnalité, s'il estime que  la  loi  qui  devrait  être  applicable  à  son  litige  ne  serait  pas  conforme  à  la Constitution.  Les  juridictions  devant  lesquelles  sont  soulevées  ladite  exception, seraient  ainsi  obligées  de  saisir  le  Conseil  Constitutionnel  et  de  surseoir  à  statuer jusqu'à ce que ce dernier se soit prononcé sur la question préjudicielle dont il est, ainsi, saisi. Si ce dernier estime la disposition inconstitutionnelle, celle-ci est écartée pour le litige en cours.
110. De son côté, la Cour suprême, la juridiction suprême de droit commun, s'assure du respect des droits de la personne sur l'ensemble du territoire du fait qu'elle est :
·  Juge en premier et dernier ressort de l'excès de pouvoir des autorités exécutives et de la légalité des actes des collectivités locales;
·  Compétente  en  dernier  ressort  du  contentieux  des  inscriptions  sur  les  listes électorales et des élections rurales, municipales et régionales;
·  Chargée de l'examen des pouvoirs en cassation pour incompétence, violation de la loi ou de la coutume dirigée contre :
·  Les arrêts et jugements rendus en dernier ressort par toutes les juridictions;
·  Les décisions des conseils d'arbitrage des conflits collectifs de travail;

·  Compétente pour les recours en cassation dirigés contre les décisions de la Cour  des  comptes  et  les  décisions  rendues  en  dernier  ressort  par  les organismes administratifs à caractère juridictionnel;
·  Amenée à se prononcer sur :
·  Les demandes de révision des arrêts;
·  Les demandes de renvoi d'une juridiction à une autre pour cause de suspicion légitime ou de sûreté publique;
·  Les demandes de prise à partie contre une Cour d'appel, une Cour d'assises ou une juridiction entière;
·  Les  contrariétés  de  jugements  ou  arrêts  rendus  en  dernier  ressort  entre  les mêmes parties et sur les mêmes moyens entre différentes juridictions.

111. La Cour suprême peut également être consultée par le Président de la République, le Président  de  l'Assemblée  nationale  ou  le  Gouvernement  sur  toute  question  juridique  se rapportant à la vie de la nation.
112. La même Loi organique permet, par la procédure du rabat d'arrêt, à toute personne de remettre aussi en cause une décision de la Cour suprême déjà frappée de l'autorité de la chose jugée.

113. En effet, le Procureur général près la Cour suprême ou les parties à un litige peuvent introduire une requête en rabat d'arrêt contre un arrêt définitif rendu par la Cour suprême lorsque  celui-ci  « est  entachée  d'une  erreur  de  procédure,  non  imputable  à  la  partie intéressée et qui a affecté la solution donnée à l'affaire par la Cour suprême. ».

114. Ladite  requête  est  examinée  par  la  Cour  suprême  en  chambres  réunies  et  les magistrats qui ont connu de l'affaire, à l'occasion de l'examen d'un pourvoi en cassation, ne prennent pas part au délibéré.
115. Cette  procédure  permet  au  justiciable  de  faire  corriger  les  erreurs  de  procédure judiciaire imputable au service public de la justice.

116. Au niveau des juridictions de droit commun, cette problématique concerne plutôt les questions  relatives  aux  droits  de  la  personne  humaine.  À  ce  titre,  l'individu  en  procès bénéficie,  en  sus  des  droits  reconnus  à  tout  homme  en  procès,  de  droits  prévus spécifiquement dans le procès pénal. Les principes directeurs applicables au procès pénal sont notamment : le principe du contradictoire, la présomption d'innocence et le principe du respect des droits de la défense.

117. Ainsi,  la  partie  civile  ou  la  personne  poursuivie  peut  bénéficier,  conformément  à l'article 10 de la Déclaration Universelle des Droits de l'Homme, de ce que « sa cause soit entendue de manière équitable et publique par  un tribunal impartial et indépendant ». En outre, la personne poursuivie a droit au respect de son intégrité physique; ce qui écarte tout acte  de  torture  dans  la  recherche  de  preuves.  Un  tel  principe  est  d'ailleurs  contenu  dans l'article 5 de la Déclaration précitée.

118. Par ailleurs, la personne poursuivie bénéficie du droit au secret de la correspondance des communications postales, télégraphiques et téléphoniques. Le droit au respect de la vie privée est également garanti.

119. De  manière  générale,  la  personne  poursuivie  bénéficie  de  l'ensemble  des prescriptions  édictées  dans  le  cadre  des  dispositions  des  conventions  internationales  en matière de Droits de l'Homme auxquelles le Sénégal est Partie et celles prévues dans le titre II  de  la  Constitution. items_en: 108. The supreme law of the country makes the judiciary the guardian of the rights and freedoms it enshrines. Judicial reform of human rights is mainly concerned with the high constitutional jurisdiction and the ordinary courts.

109. In its work of creating rights and obligations, the legislator, as well as the administrative authority, may well set limits on the content or conditions for the exercise of legal provisions relating to human rights. In such cases, the Constitutional Council may be seized for the purpose of preventing the promulgation or application of the law by means of two procedures, inter alia, by means of action and by way of exception:

· Referral by action: the Constitution of Senegal gives to the President of the Republic or to the deputies representing 1/10 e of the members of the National Assembly the power to refer to the Constitutional Council an appeal to have the conformity of a law with the Constitution checked before its promulgation. Thus, a law which ignores the provisions relating to human rights can be referred to the Constitutional Council for the purpose of such review.

· Referral by way of exception: the grantor has intended to "democratize" access to the Constitutional Council for a review of the constitutionality of the law, by means of the mechanism of referral by way of exception. This referral is indeed more open, in that it is within the reach of any person who may, under the conditions laid down by law, raise the exception of unconstitutionality, if he considers that the law which should be applicable to his dispute would not be in conformity with the Constitution. The courts before which the exception is raised would thus be obliged to refer the matter to the Constitutional Council and to stay the decision until the latter has decided on the question referred to it for a preliminary ruling. If the latter considers that the provision is unconstitutional, the latter is dismissed for the present dispute.
110. For its part, the Supreme Court, the supreme court under ordinary law, ensures that human rights are respected throughout the country by virtue of the fact that it is:
· Judge in the first and last instance of the excessive power of the executive authorities and of the legality of the acts of local authorities;
· Competency as a last resort in the litigation of voter registration and rural, municipal and regional elections;
· Responsible for examining powers in cassation for incompetence, violation of the law or custom directed against:
· Judgments and judgements rendered as a last resort by all courts;
· Decisions of collective labour dispute arbitration boards;
· Competency in cassation proceedings against decisions of the Court of Auditors and final decisions of administrative bodies of a judicial nature;
· Determined on:
· Applications for review of judgments;
· Requests for referral from one court to another on the grounds of legitimate suspicion or public security;
· Applications to take action against a Court of Appeal, a Court of Assize or an entire court;
· Contraceptions of judgements or judgments handed down as a last resort between the same parties and on the same grounds between different courts.
111. The Supreme Court may also be consulted by the President of the Republic, the President of the National Assembly or the Government on any legal matter relating to the life of the nation.
112. The same Organic Law allows any person to challenge a decision of the Supreme Court that has already been subject to res judicata through the trial proceedings.

113. Indeed, the Attorney General of the Supreme Court or the parties to a dispute may file a motion for a roll-off of a judgement against a final judgement of the Supreme Court where the judgement "is tainted by an error of procedure, not attributable to the party concerned and which has affected the solution given to the case by the Supreme Court".

114. The application is examined by the Supreme Court in the combined chambers, and the judges who have heard the case during the examination of an appeal in cassation do not take part in the deliberations.
115. This procedure enables the litigant to correct errors in judicial proceedings attributable to the public service of justice.

116. At the level of the ordinary courts, this problem is more concerned with human rights issues, in which, in addition to the rights accorded to all persons at trial, the individual is entitled to rights specifically provided for in the criminal trial, including the principle of adversarial proceedings, the presumption of innocence and the principle of respect for the rights of the defence.

117. Thus, in accordance with article 10 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the civil party or the person prosecuted may benefit from "a fair and public hearing by an impartial and independent tribunal". Moreover, the person prosecuted has the right to respect for his or her physical integrity, which precludes any act of torture in the search for evidence, and such a principle is contained in article 5 of the Declaration.
118. In addition, the accused person enjoys the right to confidentiality of correspondence in postal, telegraphic and telephone communications, and the right to privacy is also guaranteed.

119. Generally speaking, the person prosecuted enjoys all the requirements laid down in the provisions of the international human rights conventions to which Senegal is a party and those laid down in Title II of the Constitution.
